facts.fact: The Convocation of Silvermoon is a standing pressure on Sylvanas's time and patience, associated with budget reviews, clerks, and procedural drag.
A detained human mage inside the ward network is immediately treated as a political problem as well as a military one.
Jaina Proudmoore's connection to Antonidas escalates the detention from border incident to potentially broader diplomatic crisis.
Lor'themar functions as the Farstrider political shield, riding north to manage the council side while Sylvanas handles the field side.
Jaina crossed into Quel'Thalas without Kirin Tor sanction, which means her usefulness does not erase the diplomatic mess around her presence.
Under immediate threat, Sylvanas will convert a politically dangerous detainee into a controlled field asset rather than wait for clean authorization chains.
Once Millhaven's proof is secure, Sylvanas asserts emergency authority across the north road by ordering copies handed to every gate and settlement without waiting for further approval.
Chapter 6 establishes that Lordaeron command can hear a correct plague warning and still subordinate it to the visible war already demanding force, making hierarchy itself part of the disaster.
Arthas's refusal is politically important because it is framed as priority and command burden, not simple personal cruelty; future continuity should preserve that distinction.

Chapter 7 shows bureaucracy itself acting as concealment: local magistrates dismiss rat theories, records vanish upward, and useful action depends on ignored people inside the system trusting the field party faster than the hierarchy does.
Chapter 8 turns Kel'Thuzad from local rumor into an institutional wound: Antonidas recognizes the name instantly, which means the plague now carries direct Kirin Tor political and historical baggage.
Even the right institutional listener can only promise to start what he can from behind the field, making chapter 8 a continuation of structural delay rather than a clean political fix.
Corin and Havel staying local while the four-woman field unit moves on is itself a political fact about trust: useful individuals inside broken systems matter more than those systems' official tempo.
By chapter 9 the Convocation review of Sylvanas's authority is immediate and personal rather than distant administrative drag.
The Kirin Tor response has shifted into methodology review and proof language, which means Dalaran is now lagging the field in the same way Lordaeron command already was.
Chapter 10 shows House Barov handling scrutiny through controlled hospitality rather than denial, implying enough confidence and standing to absorb outside rank into a curated script.
Kirin Tor authority can force entry into the Barov surface, but not actual control over the institution beneath it.
Chapter 11 shows evidence-erasure becoming overt political pressure: verification teams find scrubbed sites and hostile local lords while Silvermoon pushes recall from behind.
A single prince can be moved faster than the larger institution around him, but that does not mean the institution itself has recovered or aligned.
Lor’themar’s dispatch confirms procedure has become a second enemy, threatening to close the field operation before it can finish.
Chapter 12 clarifies that Dalaran's necromancy ban was a civic and ethical prohibition about consent, personhood, and social trust, not a declaration that the theory itself failed.
Scholomance's deepest authority still sits below every surface host and threshold guide; rank can buy access to the institution's face without buying control over its core.
Chapter13 removes any remaining luxury of private repair before the next decision; relay urgency overrides personal reckoning the moment the trio leaves Caer Darrow.
Jaina leaves Scholomance carrying intelligence larger than the road-part she can safely say at once, proving knowledge and disclosure are now politically out of sync.

Chapter 13 shows the political face of Scholomance and the real authority beneath it are not the same thing: a Barov-affiliated scholar can host or mislead, but the deeper institution belongs to a different chain of command.
Hearthglen being overwhelmed on relay means there is no political time left for private repair; emergency movement now outranks explanation even after the core answer is won.
By chapter 14 the Convocation of Silvermoon has escalated from review pressure to formal recall, and Sylvanas answers with outright field refusal.
Antonidas's recall lands mentor-first rather than bureaucrat-first, but Jaina still refuses Dalaran in favor of the southbound mission.

Chapter 14 locks in a new political asymmetry: the protagonists now hold knowledge larger than either institution can absorb at road speed, so both institutions are effectively bypassed in favor of field action.
By chapter 15 political delay has already failed; Arthas acts only after visible disaster has made the plague impossible to leave inside paperwork or rumor.
Uther’s practical backing of Jaina’s side lane shows that battlefield authority can partially resist a prince’s tempo without actually stopping it.
Arthas claims immediate crown authority at Stratholme and treats contradiction as refusal rather than counsel.
Uther’s public refusal turns private worry about the prince into an open command rupture inside Lordaeron’s own hierarchy.
Jaina’s refusal leaves the purge without Kirin Tor moral cover and makes her split with Arthas visible to witnesses on the ground.
Chapter 18 converts Stratholme's command rupture into territorial consequence: Arthas goes north on his own chosen line while Uther rides west to carry warning toward Capital City.
Antonidas can still offer Jaina extraction after Stratholme, but the field crisis now outruns even direct mentor authority, leaving the line unresolved instead of obedient.
Chapter 19 escalates Silvermoon's pressure from recall to threatened desertion, and Sylvanas answers by openly asserting that Quel'Thalas's real threat now lies wherever Arthas has gone.
Lor'themar can buy only token mobilization and relay support to the northern harbors, proving Silvermoon politics will support the mission only at its thinnest edge.
Jaina closes the Dalaran line herself in chapter 19, turning prior refusal into visible institutional severance rather than temporary deferral.

By chapter22 Arthas has been back in Capital City for four days and the public story still treats him as the prince who ended the northern threat, showing that royal image management has outrun every private warning.
Antonidas can reach the palace margins with two Kirin Tor magi but lacks the authority to force the throne-room problem through formal channels.

Uther is physically present inside the palace sphere in chapter22 and still blocked by court protocol, confirming that even correct high-ranking opposition now lacks procedural access to the king in time.

Chapter23 proves that even correct late authority cannot simply erase appearances: Uther reaches the throne room in time to control it, but not in time to give Jaina and Sylvanas a clean exonerating witness sequence.
The death of both king and prince in the same chamber means the immediate aftermath is a succession crisis as well as a criminal scene, making testimony and custody politically unavoidable.

Victory over Arthas does not yield instant legitimacy for the protagonists; the palace aftermath demonstrates that institutions can inherit the wrong visible story even when the right people are finally in the room.
Chapter24 establishes that Lordaeron can convert a succession catastrophe into tribunal process faster than it can answer the magical danger still sitting in palace custody.
Antonidas and Uther can be politically present and still lack the authority to force the court to treat Frostmourne as the primary problem.
Silvermoon's official position is public disavowal through Vereesa even while Lor'themar independently pushes armed and magical corroboration into the city.
